text: It was a blast
Yes, I had a great time!
Dear all - this is a test to be sure you can all engage in the communities of practise! Respond to back to this post to share your ideas and vision for this community.
Following from our leadership session today please share with us in this forum, one key leadership lesson learned. which you think is relevant for your peers!

One key lesson I have learned over time, is being sure to receive open and constructive feedback from my team. I can go along thinking all is going well, with my team. But when I stop to listen - I realize that I am alone. The team has not followed, and trying to manage as they can - but with no clear direction and guidance my side.

One key leadership lesson I learned is the importance of effective communication. Clear, concise communication fosters understanding, alignment, and trust within the team, leading to better collaboration and outcomes.
Question to the group! Do you feel that you have enough information on which pooled funds apply to your organisation and your sectors?
Questions for Mustapha Teleh on Leadership and Governance as follow up from the Abuja workshop and ongoing interaction with the workshop participants

Hygiene promotion involves educating and encouraging individuals and communities to adopt practices that promote good hygiene, such as handwashing, sanitation, and proper waste disposal, to prevent the spread of diseases and maintain overall health. It\'s crucial for preventing the spread of illnesses, especially in areas with limited access to clean water and sanitation facilities.

Child Case Management refers to the process of assessing, planning, coordinating, and monitoring services and interventions to meet the individual needs of children and their families. It involves a collaborative approach involving multiple stakeholders, including social workers, healthcare providers, educators, and community organizations, to ensure that children receive comprehensive support and care

Climate change has profound effects on women and girls, particularly in developing countries like Nigeria. Here are some:

1. Disproportionate Impact: Women and girls are often disproportionately affected by climate change due to their roles in society, such as being responsible for household water and food security. they fetch firewood, and other sources of local fuel

2. Health Impacts: Women and girls are more likely to experience negative health impacts due to climate change, such as malnutrition, heat stress, and greater exposure to climate-sensitive diseases.

3. Water Scarcity: Women and girls are disproportionately affected by water scarcity caused by climate change, as they often have to travel longer distances to collect water and spend more time doing so, reducing their opportunities for education and earning an income.

4. Natural Disasters: Women and girls are at higher risk during natural disasters, such as floods and hurricanes, as they are less likely to have access to information, emergency shelters, and medical care.

5. Food Insecurity: Climate change affects the agricultural sector, which has a significant impact on food security. Women and girls are more vulnerable to food insecurity since they are often responsible for food production and procurement.

6. Education: Climate change affects access to education for girls, particularly in rural areas, where extreme weather events, water shortages, and agricultural work may interfere with their education such as in the Northeast Nigeria.

please add yours, and then we discuss what we can do as WLOs to reduce the effects and better the lives of women and girls

The insecurity caused by the activities of insurgency and herders-farmers in Northeast Nigeria has directly affected farming activities which consequently led to shortage of food in the country.  Note: we are just practicing!

I can\'t agree less, but to add that the continued institutionalization of discrimination against women and girls, labelling them as bad leaders, and the patriarchal nature of our society remain critical to the exacerbation of this disparity.

Question please: Can anyone substantiate the impact of case management in providing wholistic humanitarian assistance to children affected by conflict in Nigeria? How do we integrate case management to effective education programming especially in the midst of school safety challenges?

IYCF stands for Infant and Young Child Feeding. It encompasses the practices and behaviors related to feeding infants and young children, typically from birth up to the age of 2 years. IYCF is crucial for ensuring optimal growth, development, and health outcomes during this critical period of early childhood. Breastfeeding: Promoting and supporting exclusive breastfeeding for the first six months of life, as recommended by the World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF. Breast milk provides essential nutrients and antibodies that protect against infections and diseases, while also promoting bonding between mother and child. Complementary Feeding: Introducing safe and appropriate complementary foods alongside continued breastfeeding from 6 months of age onwards. Complementary foods should be nutritious, diverse, and age-appropriate, meeting the child\'s evolving nutritional needs as they transition to a more varied diet. Responsive Feeding: Practicing responsive feeding behaviors that involve feeding infants and young children in a nurturing and responsive manner, paying attention to their hunger and fullness cues, and encouraging self-regulation of food intake. Hygiene and Food Safety: Ensuring hygienic practices during food preparation, storage, and feeding to prevent contamination and reduce the risk of foodborne illnesses. This includes washing hands before handling food, using clean utensils and feeding containers, and storing food at safe temperatures. Nutrition Education and Counseling: Providing education and counseling to caregivers on optimal feeding practices, including the importance of breastfeeding, appropriate timing and types of complementary foods, and responsive feeding techniques. This may be delivered through individual counseling sessions, group education sessions, or community-based programs. Supportive Environments: Creating supportive environments that enable mothers and caregivers to practice optimal feeding behaviors, including access to maternity leave, workplace lactation support, breastfeeding-friendly healthcare facilities, and community support groups. Monitoring and Evaluation: Monitoring the implementation of IYCF practices and evaluating their impact on child growth, development, and health outcomes. This may involve routine growth monitoring, assessment of feeding practices, and tracking of key indicators such as exclusive breastfeeding rates and dietary diversity.

Impact of case management in providing wholistic humanitarian assistance to children affected by conflict in Nigeria includes:  Comprehensive Needs Assessment: Case management involves conducting comprehensive assessments of children\'s needs, including physical, emotional, social, and psychological well-being. This holistic approach ensures that all aspects of a child\'s situation are considered, allowing for tailored interventions that address their unique needs and vulnerabilities. Individualized Care Planning: Based on the assessment findings, case managers develop individualized care plans that outline specific goals and interventions to address the child\'s needs. These care plans take into account the child\'s age, gender, cultural background, and any pre-existing conditions, ensuring that interventions are appropriate and effective. Coordination of Services: Case managers serve as the central point of contact for coordinating services and support for children affected by conflict. They work closely with a range of stakeholders, including healthcare providers, social workers, educators, and community organizations, to ensure that children receive access to essential services such as healthcare, education, psychosocial support, and protection. Advocacy and Support: Case managers advocate for the rights and best interests of children affected by conflict, ensuring that they have access to the services and support they need to recover and thrive. This may involve advocating for access to education, healthcare, and protection services, as well as addressing any barriers or challenges that may impede a child\'s well-being. Monitoring and Follow-Up: Case management involves ongoing monitoring and follow-up to track the progress of interventions and ensure that children\'s needs are being met. Case managers regularly review care plans, assess the effectiveness of interventions, and make adjustments as needed to ensure that children receive the support they require. Empowerment and Resilience-Building: Through case management, children are empowered to actively participate in decisions about their care and well-being. Case managers provide children with opportunities to express their needs, preferences, and concerns, fostering a sense of agency and resilience in the face of adversit

Quote from bela.code12@gmail.com on May 8, 2024, 9:23 am

How can leaders foster a culture of innovation and adaptability within their organizations?

Dear Bela, Thanks for your interesting question. Leaders can forster a work climate of adaptability and innovation by employing varied strategies.
  1. The Key is aligning every staff to the Vision and mission of the organisation with emphasis on promoting contonous learning, creativity, innovations, application and adaptability. Leaders should always encourage team members to think out of the box and profer solutions based on learnings, application of experiences and allow room for mistakes.
  2. Leaders should embody innovation and adapdability in thier approach to work.
  3. Leaders should celebrate and recognize innovative ideas and always encourage innovation and risk taking.
  4. The organisational culture and behavior should promote innovation and adaptability.

Hello and good morning everyone, my question is on HR, what mechanism can an organization follow in Recruiting staff?  Thank you
Good Morning Zaman Lafiya. The 1st step is to develop a robust HR policy which will on a continous basis guide your recruitment process. The HR policy should articulate the different type of positions obtainable in the organisation and the recruitment process. Strict adherence to the HR policy is a function of organisational compliance mechhanism.

Quote from wyeahi@gmail.com on May 8, 2024, 9:27 am
Good morning once again, please I would like to know the empirical relationship between governance or leadership and success of an organization.
Dear Wyeah, Indeed there is a relationship between CSO Governance and Leadership. the Governance component considers the structure and functionality of the organisation while leadership considers the operationalization of the vision and mission of the organisation as set out by the the governance structure. Leadership is the ability of an individual or a group of people to influence and guide followers or members of an organization, society or team while Governance refers to the framework of policies, processes, and structures that guide the management and oversight of the CSOs BOT and ED roles within an organization. This governance framework is designed to ensure that the BOT and ED are effectively fulfilling their responsibilities in protecting the organization\'s assets, including its information systems, data, physical infrastructure, and personnel, from security threats and risks. Thank you

Quote from info@pro-wellness.org on May 8, 2024, 9:34 am
Good morning to you from Pro-Wellness Initiative, Mr. Teleh. It\'s a pleasure to have you here. My question revolves around strategies that women-led organizations can employ to bolster transparency and accountability within their governance structures. Could you please shed light on some effective approaches in this regard?
In my opinion, transparency and accountability goes beyond the practice of enlisting organizational core values. It have to become a culture of the organization which must be derived by the leadership. An effective approach would involve the leadership being honest with the staff about every thing that concerns them. For example, organizing an inception meeting with all staff to review the project document; workplan, budget and risk matrix. Holding weekly meetings to discuss both program and operational update where the leadership would be intentional in sharing updated of happenings at their level. It is understandable to hear concerns such as staff misusing the privileged information but providing constant generic information to all staff internally while need to know are maintained for highly sensitive issues would be great.

Quote from Ndubuisi Ezenwa on May 8, 2024, 9:27 am
A question for the Women-led organisations that participated in the Abuja sessions: were there any aspects of your organisational governance you felt needed improvement after gaining new insight from the training with Mr. Teleh? Have you been able to make changes since then? Have you had any challenges you would like to share?
After the Abuja sessions, our organization conducted a thorough review of our operational foundation. It became glaringly apparent that our board lacked the diverse skill set necessary to effectively guide us towards our organizational objectives. We identified some critical deficiencies: the absence of a comprehensive board constitution, an annual work plan for the board, an evaluation mechanism, and even a clear role and responsibilities for board members. Furthermore, there was a notable absence of a structured onboarding process for new board members, exacerbating the issue. Compounding our challenges, some existing board members were not actively contributing to our endeavors. However, buoyed by the insights gained during the Abuja sessions, we swiftly sprang into action. We convened a series of board meetings with the express purpose of assimilating the principles of effective governance and meticulously scrutinizing the deficiencies within our board structure. Recognizing the urgent imperative to fortify our team with individuals possessing the requisite skills, we successfully recruited a female board member with expertise in financial management. Concurrently, we are actively working on the development of a comprehensive board constitution. Additionally, we established a strategic team tasked with crafting the board\'s annual work plan, devising an evaluation mechanism, and designing a structured onboarding process to facilitate the seamless integration of new board members into our organization. Thank you so much PLRCAP Team.

Quote from info@pro-wellness.org on May 8, 2024, 9:34 am
Good morning to you from Pro-Wellness Initiative, Mr. Teleh. It\'s a pleasure to have you here. My question revolves around strategies that women-led organizations can employ to bolster transparency and accountability within their governance structures. Could you please shed light on some effective approaches in this regard?
Hello Pro-wellness, Trust you are well. The governance structure is the origin of accountability and transparency in any organisation. The governance team have to ensure there are sufficient policies and guidelines to facilitate the work. The governance structure should ensure there exist a system where there are clear roles and responsibilities for every function in the organisation. They also fucnetion to ensure these policies, roles and responsibilities with timelines are adequately communicated to all staff. They ensure there are oversight functions and compliance to stipulated policies, TORs and SOPs. Feedback  mechanism should be encouraged to establish the performance of the policies and also continous improvement of the system. The governance structure shoud be accountable and transparent to its self and ensure all decision making are aligned to stipulated policies and also encorage quality improvements.   Thank you

Good Morning Cynthia, thank you for being onboard to support. I have a question .

What are the common barriers to effective communication in organizations, and how can they be mitigated?

Hello dear

Nice to have u here.

Advocacy has been a way were CSOs get policies implementated or translated. Some take too long a time.

Is there a more effective way to get it done faster???

Hello Mercy Sarah,

The common barriers to effective communication within an organization include poor listening skills, lack of feedback, assumption, lack of clear communication, language barriers.

These barriers can be mitigated by the following.
  • Deliberate acts of listening. Pay attention to your colleagues. Listen to their concerns and body language. Don\'t write someone off simply because you feel he/she is not meeting work expectations. Ask questions. That person might have a stong skill meant for a different department or the person might be going through a hard time at home...Ask questions.
  • Always give and demand feedback. That is the best way for you to know if your colleagues actually understood your message. That is the best way for you to know if you would need to make any adjustments or improvements. Staff should make it a habit to acknowledge receipts of emails and also give a realistic time frame for their deliverables. The sender should also be specific of what information is needed, when it is needed and how it should be sent. When project officers/managers return to the office from the field, send in your reports immediately and it should contain the important details. To avoid unprofessional reporting, the organization should come up with a uniform report template that would demand the relevant entries needed to be filled. Also, an anonymous suggestion box can be placed in the office for people to drop in their opinions.
  • Never assume. Ask questions, carry out your findings. Do not jump into conclusions without investigating.
  • Try as much as possible to resist the urge to speak a language in the presence of people or someone who doesn\'t understand that language. This is a huge communication problem in the workplace and it causes segregation. Do not let any of your colleagues feel uncomfortable or left out. Respect people\'s feelings.
  • In-house training should be regular for every staff to be informed about the activities and achievements of the organisation. All staff should learn the organisation\'s key message, objectives, and elevator pitch.
  • Finally, organizations should learn to acknowledge the efforts of staff. Even when a boss has something to complain about a staff, start by appreciating the good achievements of that staff. And please, never criticise a staff in public. Do it in private. I can go on and on to give different scenarios 🙂, but in all you do, mutual respect should be utmost.

DKF had a failed Advocacy in 2020 during Covid 19 lockdown.In Adamawa there was a program SCHOOL ON AIR because then all the schools were locked down.

Different subject were thought on Gotel TV daily, we realize that children with hearing impairment were not carried along. DKF wanted Deaf students to also benefit from the school on air.
We decided to go for an Advocacy visit to Gotel, the Ministry of Education, Ministry of a Women Affairs and Department of Special Education.
Until schools resumed we didn\'t breakthrough

The relevant stockholder in Education we had Advocated to. We had in our Team Joint Association of Persons With Disabilities leadership. YET

Until schools Resumed, after 6 months or thereabout Hearing Impaired children never benefited.

1 year later we got a mail.

It was not a pleasant experience for DKF

Quote from debbiekaunafoundation@gmail.com on May 15, 2024, 10:03 am

Hello dear

Nice to have u here.

Advocacy has been a way were CSOs get policies implementated or translated. Some take too long a time.

Is there a more effective way to get it done faster???
In my opinion, the duration of advocacy is dependent on the kind of change it desires to see. There are instances where a simple visit to a power holder with clear-cut advocacy messages led to an immediate executive order that addressed the issue in question. Whereas there are many other cases, especially behavior change advocacy, that would definitely last so long. In my guess, behavior change advocacy would sure last a long because people have spent a large part of their lives in these norms, so changing them must be a gradual process. However, advocacy that targets decision-makers also requires a good stakeholder analysis before engagement. If your program is able to connect with the right stakeholder or win more support, especially from within the circle of influence of the target power holder, it is likely to achieve the goals of advocacy faster.

Hello Debbie,

The long process of policy implementation can be shortened by building strategic partnerships. CSOs can combine resources and expertise with sister-organisations and networks to create a more powerful and unified voice for change.

Increase publicity for the cause using media, especially social media. We all know how social media has suddenly become the refuge for people who seek justice and law makers bowing to the pressure of social media users, please leverage on this too. This means your social media handles should be a full reflection of your organization and the cause you advocate for. It should contain the relevant update information.

You can also carry out more advocacy campaigns, sending out petitions for the public to sign, community mobilisation. However, never under the power of social media.

Quote from debbiekaunafoundation@gmail.com on May 15, 2024, 11:27 am
DKF had a failed Advocacy in 2020 during Covid 19 lockdown.In Adamawa there was a program SCHOOL ON AIR because then all the schools were locked down. Different subject were thought on Gotel TV daily, we realize that children with hearing impairment were not carried along. DKF wanted Deaf students to also benefit from the school on air. We decided to go for an Advocacy visit to Gotel, the Ministry of Education, Ministry of a Women Affairs and Department of Special Education. Until schools resumed we didn\'t breakthrough The relevant stockholder in Education we had Advocated to. We had in our Team Joint Association of Persons With Disabilities leadership. YET Until schools Resumed, after 6 months or thereabout Hearing Impaired children never benefited. 1 year later we got a mail. It was not a pleasant experience for DKF
It is quite unfortunate, but I think sometimes we need to come up with a proposed solution in terms of advocacy. Yes, the hearing-impaired children are left out, and you met the relevant stakeholders. The understanding of the posture of your stakeholders matters as well; government stakeholders are not so enthusiastic about anything that will mean additional cost to the already designed program (especially when they have settled their cuts). But, when you come up with a proposed solution, like having a trained sign language professional that you can deploy to work with them on the sign communication during such a program, it means that they will not bear much of the cost of engaging such a professional. Except to be proactive enough to request that at least the cost of transport for such a person  be covered, the joint team advocating for such a change will make the necessary arrangements to cover the professional fee since the production of episodes is not daily. Just my thoughts!
